blocks:
Ayahs

هَلْ أَتَىٰكَ حَدِيثُ ٱلْغَـٰشِيَةِ (١)
Did you receive the account of the Enveloper? (1)
وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ خَـٰشِعَةٌ (٢)
Some faces on that day will be humbled, (2)
عَامِلَةٌ نَّاصِبَةٌ (٣)
wrought-up and weary: (3)
تَصْلَىٰ نَارًا حَامِيَةً (٤)
they will enter a scorching fire (4)
تُسْقَىٰ مِنْ عَيْنٍ ءَانِيَةٍ (٥)
and made to drink from a boiling spring. (5)
لَّيْسَ لَهُمْ طَعَامٌ إِلَّا مِن ضَرِيعٍ (٦)
They will have no food except cactus, (6)
لَّا يُسْمِنُ وَلَا يُغْنِى مِن جُوعٍ (٧)
neither nourishing, nor of avail against hunger. (7)
وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ نَّاعِمَةٌ (٨)
Some faces on that day will be joyous, (8)
لِّسَعْيِهَا رَاضِيَةٌ (٩)
pleased with their endeavour, (9)
فِى جَنَّةٍ عَالِيَةٍ (١٠)
in a lofty garden, (10)
لَّا تَسْمَعُ فِيهَا لَـٰغِيَةً (١١)
where they will not hear any vain talk. (11)
فِيهَا عَيْنٌ جَارِيَةٌ (١٢)
In it is a flowing spring (12)
فِيهَا سُرُرٌ مَّرْفُوعَةٌ (١٣)
and raised couches, (13)
وَأَكْوَابٌ مَّوْضُوعَةٌ (١٤)
with goblets set, (14)
وَنَمَارِقُ مَصْفُوفَةٌ (١٥)
and cushions laid out in an array, (15)
وَزَرَابِىُّ مَبْثُوثَةٌ (١٦)
and carpets spread out. (16)
أَفَلَا يَنظُرُونَ إِلَى ٱلْإِبِلِ كَيْفَ خُلِقَتْ (١٧)
Do they not observe the camel, [to see] how it has been created? (17)
وَإِلَى ٱلسَّمَآءِ كَيْفَ رُفِعَتْ (١٨)
and the sky, how it has been raised? (18)
وَإِلَى ٱلْجِبَالِ كَيْفَ نُصِبَتْ (١٩)
and the mountains, how they have been set? (19)
وَإِلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ كَيْفَ سُطِحَتْ (٢٠)
and the earth, how it has been surfaced? (20)
فَذَكِّرْ إِنَّمَآ أَنتَ مُذَكِّرٌ (٢١)
So admonish—for you are only an admonisher, (21)
لَّسْتَ عَلَيْهِم بِمُصَيْطِرٍ (٢٢)
and not a taskmaster over them— (22)
إِلَّا مَن تَوَلَّىٰ وَكَفَرَ (٢٣)
except him who turns back and disbelieves. (23)
فَيُعَذِّبُهُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْعَذَابَ ٱلْأَكْبَرَ (٢٤)
Him Allah will punish with the greatest punishment. (24)
إِنَّ إِلَيْنَآ إِيَابَهُمْ (٢٥)
Indeed to Us will be their return. (25)
ثُمَّ إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا حِسَابَهُم (٢٦)
Then, indeed, their reckoning will lie with Us. (26)

(Commentary)

This is a Surah of warning and glad tidings, describing the Enveloper (الغاشية) — the Day of Resurrection that encompasses all people. It describes the state of people therein regarding their division into two groups: the felicitous and the wretched, and their settling into what has been prepared for them of Paradise and the Fire. It concludes with His command to him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to remind the people of the various modes of Lordly governance (التدبير الربوبي) in the world that point toward His Lordship (الربوبية) — the absolute sovereignty and nurturing care of Allah over His creation — over them, and their return to Him for the reckoning of their deeds.

The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the context of its verses.

His saying, the Exalted: هَلْ أَتاكَ حَدِيثُ الْغاشِيَةِ "Did there come to you the story of the Enveloper?" (1) This is an interrogation intended to aggrandize and emphasize the gravity of the matter. What is meant by the Enveloper (الغاشية) is the Day of Resurrection; it is named thus because it covers (تغشى) the people and encompasses them, as He said: وَحَشَرْناهُمْ فَلَمْ نُغادِرْ مِنْهُمْ أَحَداً "and We shall muster them, and We will not leave out any one of them" (18:47). Or it is because it covers the people with its terrors suddenly, as has been said; or because it covers the faces of the disbelievers with punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ خاشِعَةٌ "Some faces on that day will be humbled," (2) meaning abased by the grief and punishment covering them. Humility (الخشوع) — the state of submissiveness and lowliness — actually belongs to the owners of the faces, but it is attributed to the faces because humility and abasement manifest therein.

His saying, the Exalted: عامِلَةٌ ناصِبَةٌ "laboring, weary," (3) Weariness (النصب) is exhaustion. "Laboring" (عامِلَةٌ) is a predicate after a predicate for "faces," and likewise are His sayings: "weary," "entering," "given to drink," and "they shall have no [food]." What is meant by their labor and weariness—by the context of their juxtaposition with the description of the people of Paradise that follows in His saying: "well-pleased with their endeavor"—is their labor in the world and their weariness in the Hereafter. For man only labors in the world to achieve felicity and attain his objective, but their labor was a vain wandering that profits them nothing, as the Exalted said: وَقَدِمْنا إِلى ما عَمِلُوا مِنْ عَمَلٍ فَجَعَلْناهُ هَباءً مَنْثُوراً "Then We shall attend to the works they have done and then render them as scattered dust" (25:23). Thus, nothing returns to them from their labor except weariness and exhaustion, unlike the people of Paradise, for they are well-pleased with the endeavor they undertook in the world because it led them to Paradise and rest.

It has been said: It means they are laboring in the Fire and weary therein, for they deal with the various types of punishment they are tormented with and become exhausted by that.

It has also been said: It means they were laboring in the world with sins and are weary in the Fire on the Day of Resurrection.

His saying, the Exalted: تَصْلى ناراً حامِيَةً "entering a scorching fire," (4) meaning they are bound to a fire at the ultimate degree of heat.

His saying, the Exalted: تُسْقى مِنْ عَيْنٍ آنِيَةٍ "given to drink from a boiling spring." (5) meaning hot, reaching the limit of its temperature.

His saying, the Exalted: لَيْسَ لَهُمْ طَعامٌ إِلَّا مِنْ ضَرِيعٍ لا يُسْمِنُ وَلا يُغْنِي مِنْ جُوعٍ "They shall have no food except cactus, which neither fattens nor avails against hunger." (6-7) It is said that the cactus (الضريع) is a type of thorn called shibriq; the people of the Hijaz call it dari' when it dries. It is the foulest and most loathsome of foods, which no beast grazes upon. Perhaps naming what is in the Fire by this name is merely due to a similarity in form and characteristic.

His saying, the Exalted: وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ ناعِمَةٌ "Some faces on that day will be joyful," (8) derived from softness (النعومة), so it is a metaphor for the radiance and joy apparent on the skin, as He said: تَعْرِفُ فِي وجُوهِهِمْ نَضْرَةَ النَّعِيمِ "You will perceive in their faces the freshness of bliss" (83:24). Or it is from blessing (النعمة), meaning they are living in ease. It is said that it was not joined by a conjunction to His saying: "Some faces on that day will be humbled" to indicate the complete distinction (البينونة) between the states of the two groups.

His saying, the Exalted: لِسَعْيِها راضِيَةٌ "well-pleased with their endeavor," (9) The preposition lam is for emphasis. What is meant by endeavor (السعي) is their striving in the world through righteous deeds (العمل الصالح). The meaning is that they are pleased with their endeavor—which is their righteous work—since they have been rewarded for it with a beautiful reward.

His saying, the Exalted: فِي جَنَّةٍ عالِيَةٍ "in a lofty garden," (10) until His saying: وَزَرابِيُّ مَبْثُوثَةٌ "and cushions scattered about." (16) What is meant by its loftiness is the elevation of its degrees, its nobility, its majesty, and the abundance of its life. For therein is a life with which there is no death, a pleasure mixed with no pain, and a joy into which no grief or sorrow enters; they have therein more than they wish for.

His saying: لا تَسْمَعُ فِيها لاغِيَةً "where they will not hear any vain talk," (11) meaning those faces will not hear in Paradise any idle word that has no benefit.

His saying: فِيها عَيْنٌ جارِيَةٌ "in it is a flowing spring," (12) What is meant by "spring" is the genus, for the Exalted has enumerated various springs therein in His speech, such as Salsabil, the Pure Drink (الشراب الطهور), and others.

His saying: فِيها سُرُرٌ مَرْفُوعَةٌ "in it are raised couches," (13) Surur is the plural of sarir (couch); in their elevation is the majesty of the one sitting upon them. وَأَكْوابٌ مَوْضُوعَةٌ "and goblets set," (14) Akwab is the plural of kub, which is a jug that has no spout or handle, used for drink. وَنَمارِقُ مَصْفُوفَةٌ "and cushions arrayed," (15) Namariq is the plural of numruqah, which is a pillow; their being "arrayed" means they are placed in the gathering such that they are joined to one another in the manner of luxurious assemblies in the world. وَزَرابِيُّ مَبْثُوثَةٌ "and carpets spread out." (16) Zarabi is the plural of zaribah (with a fathah, kasrah, or dammah on the zay), which is a luxurious carpet; their being "scattered" means they are spread out for sitting upon.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَلا يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَى الْإِبِلِ كَيْفَ خُلِقَتْ "Do they not sight the camels, how they were created?" (17) After finishing the description of the Enveloper and explaining the state of the two groups—the believers and the disbelievers—He followed it with a summary indication of the Lordly governance (التدبير الربوبي) which speaks of His Lordship, the Exalted, which necessitates the obligation of His worship. The corollary of this is the reckoning of deeds and the rewarding of the believer for his faith and the disbeliever for his disbelief; the time and place for this is the Enveloper.

He first called them to look at the camels, how they were created? And how Allah, the Glorified, fashioned a land devoid of life and lacking consciousness into this wondrous form in its limbs, faculties, and actions? He then subjected them to them so they might benefit from riding them, their burdens, their meat, their udders, their skins, and their wool—even their urine and dung. Is all of this a coincidental agreement not intended for its own sake?

The specific mention of camels is because the Surah is Meccan, and the first to whom it was recited were the desert Arabs, for whom keeping camels was a pillar of their livelihood.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِلَى السَّماءِ كَيْفَ رُفِعَتْ "and the heaven, how it was raised?" (18) and adorned with the sun, the moon, and the rest of the luminous stars, with all the benefits they contain for the inhabitants of the earth. He placed beneath it the air, which animals are compelled to use for their breathing.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِلَى الْجِبالِ كَيْفَ نُصِبَتْ "and the mountains, how they were set?" (19) which are the pegs of the earth that prevent its shaking, the storehouses of water from which springs and rivers gush forth, and the preservers of minerals.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِلَى الْأَرْضِ كَيْفَ سُطِحَتْ "and the earth, how it was surfaced?" (20) meaning spread out and leveled, so it became suitable for human habitation, and transport, travel, and most of the industrial activities of man were made easy therein.

These are universal governances (تدبيرات كلية) attributed to Him, the Exalted, without doubt. Thus, He is the Lord of the heaven and the earth and what is between them; He is the Lord of the human world. It is obligatory upon them to take Him as Lord, to unify Him, and to worship Him. Before them is the Enveloper, which is the Day of Reckoning and Reward.

His saying, the Exalted: فَذَكِّرْ إِنَّما أَنْتَ مُذَكِّرٌ "So remind [them], for you are only a reminder;" (21) This is a derivation from what preceded. The meaning is: since Allah, the Glorified, is their Lord and there is no lord besides Him, and before them is the Day of Reckoning and Reward for whoever among them believes or disbelieves, then remind them of that.

His saying: إِنَّما أَنْتَ مُذَكِّرٌ "for you are only a reminder" is an explanation that his function—as a Messenger—is the reminder (التذكرة), in the hope that they might respond and believe without compulsion or coercion.

His saying, the Exalted: لَسْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ بِمُصَيْطِرٍ "you are not a taskmaster over them." (22) The musaytir (المصيطر) — originally written with a sin (المسيطر) — is the one who has absolute authority. The sentence is an explanation and interpretation of His saying: "for you are only a reminder."

His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا مَنْ تَوَلَّى وَكَفَرَ "except him who turns away and disbelieves," (23) This is an exception from the omitted object of His previous saying: "So remind." The estimation is: "So remind the people, except those among them who turn away from the reminder and disbelieve," for reminding them would be futile and of no benefit. It is known that turning away and disbelief only occur after the reminder, so what is negated by the exception is the reminder after the reminder. It is as if it were said: Remind them and persist in the reminder, except for the one whom you have reminded and who then turned away and disbelieved; you are not obliged to persist in reminding him, rather turn away from him, and Allah will punish him with the greatest punishment.

Thus, His saying: فَذَكِّرْ until He said إِلَّا مَنْ تَوَلَّى وَكَفَرَ فَيُعَذِّبُهُ اللهُ الْعَذابَ الْأَكْبَرَ is similar in meaning to His saying: فَذَكِّرْ إِنْ نَفَعَتِ الذِّكْرى until He said وَيَتَجَنَّبُهَا الْأَشْقَى الَّذِي يَصْلَى النَّارَ الْكُبْرى "So remind, if the reminder is beneficial... but it will be avoided by the most wretched, who will enter the Great Fire" (87:9-12), the explanation of which has already passed.

It has been said: The exception is from the pronoun in "over them" (عَلَيْهِمْ) in His saying: "you are not a taskmaster over them." The meaning would be: You are not an authority over them, except over those who turn away from the reminder and persist in disbelief, for Allah will soon give you authority over them and command you to jihad, so you will fight them and kill them.

It has also been said: The exception is disconnected (منقطع). The meaning would be: You are not an authority over them, but whoever turns away and disbelieves among them, Allah will punish him with the greatest punishment. The first view we presented is more preponderant and closer to the meaning.

His saying, the Exalted: فَيُعَذِّبُهُ اللهُ الْعَذابَ الْأَكْبَرَ "then Allah will punish him with the greatest punishment." (24) This is the punishment of Hell. The verse, as mentioned, corresponds to His saying in Surah al-A'la: الَّذِي يَصْلَى النَّارَ الْكُبْرى "who will enter the Great Fire."

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ إِلَيْنا إِيابَهُمْ "Indeed to Us is their return," (25) Iyyab (الإياب) is the return. "To Us" (إِلَيْنا) is the predicate of inna; it was placed first for emphasis and to maintain the rhyme of the verses, not for restriction (الحصر), as no one claims that people return to other than Allah, the Glorified. The verse is in the position of providing the reason (التعليل) for the punishment mentioned in the previous verse.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ إِنَّ عَلَيْنا حِسابَهُمْ "then indeed with Us is their reckoning." (26) The discussion regarding this is the same as the discussion regarding the previous verse.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma', it is narrated that Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "Every Nasibi (ناصب) — one who harbors enmity toward the Ahl al-Bayt — even if he worships and strives, will end up at this verse: عامِلَةٌ ناصِبَةٌ تَصْلى ناراً حامِيَةً (laboring, weary, entering a scorching fire)."

I say: It was also narrated in Thawab al-A'mal with a chain of transmission, and its wording is: "Every Nasibi, even if he worships and strives, will end up at this destination: عامِلَةٌ ناصِبَةٌ تَصْلى ناراً حامِيَةً."

In the same source, narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "الضريع شيء في النار يشبه الشوك ـ أمر من الصبر وأنتن من الجيفة ـ وأشد حرا من النار سماه الله الضريع" — "The cactus (الضريع) is something in the Fire that resembles thorns—more bitter than aloe, more foul-smelling than a carcass, and hotter than the Fire; Allah named it dari'."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: لا تَسْمَعُ فِيها لاغِيَةً (where they will not hear any vain talk), he said: "Jesting and lying."

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: لَسْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ بِمُصَيْطِرٍ (you are not a taskmaster over them), he said: "Neither a guardian nor a scribe over them."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, it is recorded by Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ahmad, Abd ibn Humayd, Muslim, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Majah, Ibn Jarir, al-Hakim, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Asma' wa al-Sifat, from Jabir, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "أمرت أن أقاتل الناس حتى يقولوا : لا إله إلا الله ـ فإذا قالوها عصموا مني دماءهم وأموالهم إلا بحقها ـ وحسابهم على الله" — "I have been commanded to fight the people until they say: 'There is no god but Allah.' When they say it, they have protected their blood and their wealth from me, except by its right, and their reckoning is with Allah." Then he recited: فَذَكِّرْ إِنَّما أنتَ مُذَكِّرٌ لَسْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ بِمُصَيْطِرٍ (So remind [them], for you are only a reminder; you are not a taskmaster over them).

I say: There is no indication in the narration that the exception is from the pronoun in "over them" (عَلَيْهِمْ), as is apparent.

In the same source, in a narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا مَنْ تَوَلَّى وَكَفَرَ (except him who turns away and disbelieves), he said: "He means the one who does not take heed and does not believe you—and denies My Lordship and is ungrateful for My blessing. فَيُعَذِّبُهُ اللهُ الْعَذابَ الْأَكْبَرَ (then Allah will punish him with the greatest punishment) means the harsh, severe, and everlasting [punishment]. إِنَّ إِلَيْنا إِيابَهُمْ (Indeed to Us is their return) means their destination. ثُمَّ إِنَّ عَلَيْنا حِسابَهُمْ (then indeed with Us is their reckoning) means their reward/punishment."

In al-Nahj: He (peace be upon him) was asked: "How will Allah take account of the creation despite their great number?" He replied: "كما يرزقهم على كثرتهم" — "Just as He provides for them despite their great number." It was asked: "How will He take account of them when they do not see Him?" He replied: "كما يرزقهم ولا يرونه" — "Just as He provides for them while they do not see Him."

In the same source, Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: "كل أمة يحاسبها إمام زمانها ، ويعرف الأئمة أولياءهم وأعداءهم بسيماهم وهو قوله : وَعَلَى الْأَعْرافِ رِجالٌ يَعْرِفُونَ كُلًّا بِسِيماهُمْ" — "Every nation will be brought to account by the Imam of its time, and the Imams recognize their friends and their enemies by their marks, and that is His saying: 'And on the Elevations will be men who recognize each by their mark' (7:46)..." to the end of the hadith.

I say: The clarification of the meaning of this hadith has already passed in the commentary on the verse from Surah al-A'raf. This meaning was also narrated in al-Basa'ir from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) with a chain of transmission, in al-Kafi from Imam al-Baqir and Imam al-Kadhim (peace be upon them), and in al-Faqih from Imam al-Hadi (peace be upon him) in the Ziyarah al-Jamiah.
